caveat: NOTE: This is a statistical cross-check, not a replacement for interpretive coding. Topic models produce word clusters, not themes.

theme.name: Survival Economics: Business Models, Revenue, and Market Position in the AI Era

results.text: You think about You think about profits, right? Always an easy one. You think about customer kind of NPS. results.document: 2025-10-08 - Vilas Dhar Why the Future of Journalism Is Still Human.txt
results.text: It's not about bringing in an AI technology. It's about doing exactly what you described, which is having a fundamental reckoning with What are we as an organization in a world where many of the assumptions we have are being tested and challenged by the broad-based adoption of AI in the world around us? What's our role in shepherding those core journalistic virtues in a world where attention is becoming fragmented, where people are finding fundamentally new ways of accessing and processing information? We're adversarial and bad actors are using AI in ways that are deeply kind of convoluted and manipulative. It requires an organizational reckoning and a potential for organizational visioning to ask who do we aspire to be in an AI-enabled world? And to me, that's a much more interesting conversation than whether we should pick vendor A or vendor B in order to do X task. Exactly. Bringing that back actually into the newsroom One of the other big dilemmas that's there is related to the env

results.text: And what came out of that two-day session was a plan that turned into an agreement that they would help fund embedded AI engineering fellows at multiple news organizations. That's what I find fascinating about this program. It's sort of a co-development model. with newsrooms coming together and really collaborating. But I have to ask you right at the top about the conversations that a lot of newsroom leaders and editors often have of Should we even trust these tech companies? results.text: But also on their capacity to evaluate these new and incoming tools to ask questions about what they need to do to secure them. And then to intentionally create things like sandbox environments that are test beds where journalists can go out and try and play with these tools without creating the risk of new vulnerabilities. So that's kind of the phase two approach, and that's where organizations need to very quickly develop a new muscle and capacity to create ecosystems of AI exploration so that those innovators in the field can do what they need to. And there's a third phase to this work, which is, you know, if we started from okay, we're playing around with tools to just get to know them, we're now using the tools that exist to do what we want to do. results.document: 2026-01-27 - Melissa Bell, Aron Pilhofer, Mark Chonofsky & David Chivers Chicago Public Media on Building AI Tools That Serve the Audience.txt
results.text: Well, Melissa, I'd love to start with you to just compare where has the Chicago public media been from The time you started there with the Lenfest AI Collaborative Program, to where you've seen it, especially over the past year in 2025, how has it grown in its endeavors with AI. Sure. I started here a year and three months ago and when I joined there was some interest within the organization to experiment with AI. But as with many local and public media organizations, resources are tight. We're we don't have a lot of room for experimentation. We need to be very careful about how we're making our spend reach across editorial product maintenance, et cetera, et cetera. So there wasn't a lot of deep newsroom. revenue side, business side, product side, experimentation with AI. That of course puts us further behind the transformation that's happening around the world. And this project really allowed us to kick start our efforts to learn how we can apply this technology to our work. So from a
